Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
Off Calendar Sep-08-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS TOSHIBA CORPORATION and TOSHIBA MEMORY CORPORATION's Motion To Quash Service Of Summons as follows: Defendants Toshiba Corporation and Toshiba Memory Corporation's motion to quash is denied. The court has specific personal jurisdiction over both Toshiba defendants and can also exercise jurisdiction over both defendants based on consent of Toshiba Corporation which also applies to its subsidiary Toshiba Memory Corporation. Specific jurisdiction exists over both defendants because they purposefully directed their conduct towards California when they prevented the California Western Digital employees, who previously had access to Toshiba's databases, from accessing that information. This purposeful conduct with knowledge that it would cause harm in this state suffices to impose specific personal jurisdiction over both defendants. Plaintiff SanDisk LLC and Toshiba Corporation also agreed that any dispute involving the joint venture Master and Operating Agreements would be resolved by binding arbitration in San Francisco. By so agreeing, Toshiba Corporation purposefully availed itself of the benefits and privileges of California. (Fireman's Fund Ins. v. National Bank of Cooperatives (1996) 103 F.3d 888, 894). This lawsuit is related to or arises out of Toshiba Corporation's entering into an agreement providing for arbitration in this county and the assertion of jurisdiction comports with fair play and substantial justice. Toshiba Corporation also consented to jurisdiction per CCP 1293 as the parties agreed to arbitration in San Francisco. Even if CCP 1293 does not technically apply to this case because the agreements were made in Japan, the Toshiba defendants consented to personal jurisdiction in California. "A person's consent to jurisdiction 'may be expressed by words or by conduct.' Agreeing to resolve a particular dispute in a specific jurisdiction, for example, is one means of expressing consent to personal jurisdiction of courts in the forum state for purposes of that dispute." (Szynalski v. Sup. Crt. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1, 7-8 (citation omitted)). The California Arbitration Act contemplates that arbitration petitions and other requests for related relief will be filed in the county where the arbitration is venued. (CCP 1292(a); 1292.2; 1292.6.) Critical to this analysis is that SanDisk's lawsuit solely seeks provisional relief per CCP 1281.8(b) pending the arbitration to effectuate the arbitration. This court is not asserting jurisdiction to resolve the underlying merits of the case or any other matter. This distinguishes the cases cited by the Toshiba defendants such as Eclipse Aerospace, Inc. v. Star 7, LLC (ND IL 2016 WL 901297 and Weinberg & Co., P.A. v. Beasley Food Serv. (CD CA 2008) 2008 WL 450004. [After hearing, the Court signed the proposed order submitted by email.] Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Reporter: Reyes F. Hunter, CSR#6576, rhunter@sftc.org.
